method0.generated_article:
==Geography==

Nishinoshima (西之島, “western island”) is a volcanic island in the Ogasawara Islands of Japan, administered as part of Ogasawara Village, Tokyo Metropolis. It lies in the Philippine Sea at approximately 27°14′N, 140°52′E, about 130 km west of Chichi-jima and roughly 940–1,000 km south of central Tokyo.

The island is isolated, uninhabited, and has no permanent facilities, harbor, roads, or freshwater sources. Its coastline, shape, and area have changed repeatedly as a result of volcanic eruptions, lava flows, wave erosion, and the collapse or rebuilding of volcanic cones. Before the 1970s, Nishinoshima was a very small island, but eruptions in 1973–1974 and especially from 2013 onward greatly expanded its land area.

By the early 2020s, Nishinoshima covered approximately 4 km², though the precise figure varies with continuing volcanic activity and coastal erosion. The island’s highest point has also changed with eruptions, with recent volcanic cones reaching well over 100 m above sea level. Much of the surface consists of young lava flows, volcanic ash, cinders, and unstable coastal cliffs.

Nishinoshima is part of the wider Ogasawara archipelago, a remote oceanic island group noted for volcanic landforms, high endemism, and ecological isolation. Unlike the main inhabited Ogasawara Islands, Nishinoshima is a recently formed and actively changing volcanic island, making it one of Japan’s most dynamic landforms.

==Geology==

Nishinoshima is the exposed summit of a large submarine volcano on the Izu–Bonin–Mariana Arc, a volcanic chain formed by the subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Philippine Sea Plate. The volcano rises from the deep seafloor of the Philippine Sea, where the surrounding ocean depths reach several thousand metres.

The island is composed mainly of andesitic to basaltic-andesitic volcanic rocks. Its eruptions have included both explosive and effusive phases. Explosive activity has produced ash, scoria, volcanic bombs, and steam-driven eruptions caused by the interaction of magma with seawater. Effusive activity has produced lava flows that repeatedly enlarged the island by advancing into the sea.

Nishinoshima is scientifically significant because its lavas provide insight into magma generation in island-arc settings. Studies of its volcanic rocks have shown relatively primitive arc magmas, making the volcano important for understanding the early stages of continental crust formation and the development of volcanic island arcs.

The island’s present form is the result of rapid construction by lava flows followed by marine erosion. Newly formed lava deltas are often unstable and may collapse into the sea. Ash deposits are easily reworked by wind and rain, while waves cut steep cliffs into the island’s margins. As a result, maps of Nishinoshima become outdated quickly during active eruptive periods.

==Eruptive History==

Nishinoshima was known historically as a small, low island before modern eruptions transformed it. Its first well-documented modern eruption occurred in 1973. During this eruption, submarine vents near the original island produced a new volcanic islet. The eruption initially involved steam explosions, ash, and floating pumice, followed by lava flows. By 1974, the newly formed land had connected with the older island, creating an enlarged Nishinoshima.

After the 1973–1974 eruption, the island remained largely quiet for several decades, though minor volcanic or hydrothermal activity may have occurred offshore. The next major phase began in November 2013, when the Japan Coast Guard observed a new eruption southeast of the existing island. A small new islet formed, informally referred to as “Niijima” (“new island”), but it soon merged with Nishinoshima as lava flows continued.

The 2013–2015 eruption dramatically expanded the island. Lava flows repeatedly entered the sea, building new coastlines and covering much of the older land surface. The eruption created a central cone and extensive black lava fields. By the end of this eruptive phase, Nishinoshima had grown many times larger than its pre-2013 size.

Further eruptions occurred in 2017 and again from 2019 to 2020. The 2019–2020 activity was especially vigorous, with large ash plumes, lava fountains, and substantial new lava flows. Ashfall darkened the island, and the central cone increased in height. Lava flows extended the coastline, while explosive activity deposited thick layers of tephra over older surfaces.

Intermittent volcanic activity has continued in the years since, including episodes of discolored seawater, ash emissions, and small explosive events. Because Nishinoshima remains an active volcano, access is restricted and monitoring is conducted mainly by aircraft, satellites, and maritime observation.

==Climate==

Nishinoshima has a warm oceanic subtropical climate influenced by the surrounding Philippine Sea. There is no permanent weather station on the island, so its climate is inferred from nearby Ogasawara observations, particularly those from Chichi-jima.

Temperatures are mild to warm throughout the year. Winters are generally frost-free, while summers are hot, humid, and moderated by the ocean. The surrounding seas help reduce seasonal temperature extremes. Rain falls throughout the year, with wetter periods often associated with the early-summer rainy season and autumn storm activity.

The island is exposed to strong winds, salt spray, tropical storms, and typhoons. These factors strongly influence erosion, soil formation, and biological colonization. Heavy rain can cut channels into loose ash and cinder deposits, while storm waves reshape beaches and cliffs. Drought and salt stress also affect the establishment of plants on newly formed volcanic surfaces.

Because Nishinoshima lacks high mountains, forests, and freshwater systems, its local climate is dominated by marine exposure. Surface conditions on young lava flows can be harsh, with high daytime temperatures, little shade, unstable substrate, and limited moisture retention.

==Ecology==

Nishinoshima is an important natural laboratory for the study of ecological succession on newly formed land. Before the recent eruptions, the older island supported sparse vegetation and nesting seabirds. Much of this habitat was buried or altered by lava and ash after 2013, creating a largely barren volcanic landscape.

The recolonization of Nishinoshima is of particular scientific interest because the island has no permanent human population and is remote from other landmasses. Organisms arrive by wind, ocean currents, driftwood, floating debris, birds, and possibly storms. Seabirds play an especially important role by transporting seeds, invertebrates, and nutrients through guano.

Brown boobies and other seabirds have been observed using the island, particularly on remnant older surfaces and relatively stable coastal areas. Their nesting and roosting activity can accelerate soil development by adding organic matter and nutrients to otherwise sterile volcanic material. Over time, seabird colonies may help create conditions suitable for plants and terrestrial invertebrates.

Plant colonization is slow and uneven. Species that can tolerate salt spray, drought, high temperatures, and nutrient-poor substrates are most likely to establish first. Coastal plants with buoyant seeds or seeds dispersed by birds have the greatest chance of reaching the island. However, repeated eruptions can destroy developing vegetation and reset ecological succession.

The island’s isolation and lack of introduced mammals are ecologically important. Rats, cats, goats, and other invasive species have caused major damage on many oceanic islands, but Nishinoshima’s active volcanism and restricted access have so far limited direct human-related biological introductions. For conservationists, the island offers a rare opportunity to observe how a new oceanic island ecosystem develops with minimal human interference.

==Human History==

Nishinoshima was known to mariners before the modern volcanic eruptions and appeared on some Western charts under the name Rosario Island. It was later incorporated into Japanese territory along with the Ogasawara Islands. The Japanese name Nishinoshima means “western island,” reflecting its position west of the main Ogasawara island groups.

The island has never supported permanent settlement. Its small size, isolation, lack of freshwater, and volcanic activity made habitation impractical. Historically, it was primarily of interest to navigators, surveyors, and scientists. After the 1973–1974 eruption, it became a subject of geological and biological research because new land had formed adjacent to an older island.

The 2013 eruption attracted wide public attention in Japan and internationally. At first, some observers expected the newly formed islet to erode away, as many small volcanic islands do. Instead, continued lava output caused it to grow rapidly and merge with the older Nishinoshima. The eruption became one of the best-observed examples of rapid island formation in modern times.

Because the island is active and dangerous, landing is generally restricted. Japanese government agencies, including the Japan Coast Guard, the Japan Meteorological Agency, and scientific institutions, monitor the island by aircraft, ship, and satellite. Observations are used to track volcanic hazards, coastline changes, eruption plumes, and ecological recovery.

==Administration and Protection==

Nishinoshima is administered by Ogasawara Village, which is part of Tokyo Metropolis, despite being nearly 1,000 km from mainland Japan. Like the rest of the Ogasawara Islands, it falls within a remote maritime region of significant natural and scientific value.

The island lies within the broader protected and monitored environment of the Ogasawara Islands, which are recognized for their distinctive oceanic ecosystems and volcanic origins. Human access is tightly controlled, partly because of volcanic danger and partly because of the need to prevent disturbance to seabirds and the accidental introduction of invasive species.

Scientific visits, when permitted, are carefully planned. Researchers must consider unstable ground, toxic volcanic gases, falling ash, coastal collapse, and sudden eruptive activity. Because of these risks, many observations are made remotely using aerial photography, satellite imagery, drones, and ship-based surveys.

==Scientific Importance==

Nishinoshima is significant for volcanology, geomorphology, ecology, and conservation biology. Few places allow scientists to observe the birth and growth of an oceanic volcanic island in such detail. The island’s rapid expansion since 2013 has provided valuable data on lava flow emplacement, shoreline formation, volcanic cone growth, and erosion by waves and storms.

For geologists, Nishinoshima offers a modern example of island-arc volcanism. Its magma chemistry helps researchers understand how subduction-zone magmas form and evolve. The island’s eruptions also illustrate the interaction between lava, seawater, and explosive volcanic processes.

For biologists, Nishinoshima is a rare site where primary succession can be observed almost from the beginning. The arrival of seabirds, plants, insects, and microbes can be studied in relation to distance from source islands, ocean currents, wind patterns, and volcanic disturbance. Because eruptions repeatedly reset parts of the island, Nishinoshima provides multiple stages of colonization within a single location.

The island is also important for studying how new land becomes stable or disappears. Some newly formed volcanic islands are rapidly destroyed by waves, while others persist and become established islands. Nishinoshima has survived and expanded because of repeated lava supply, but its coast remains highly changeable. Its future size and shape will depend on the balance between volcanic construction and marine erosion.

method1.generated_article:
==Geography==
Nishinoshima is a volcanic island in the Ogasawara island chain, about 600 miles south of Tokyo.[2]  
The island is located at 27°14′49″ N, 140°52′28″ E, with an elevation point measured by the Japan Meteorological Agency at 25 m.[3]  
A historical survey described Nishinoshima as approximately 650 m long from southwest to northeast and approximately 200 m wide, with an area of about 77,000 m².[3]  
Several reefs were recorded on the northeast side of the island.[3]  

==Geological setting==
Nishinoshima is an andesitic volcanic island situated on the summit of a large submarine volcano approximately 30 km in diameter.[3]  
The island is part of the volcanic Ogasawara region, where volcanic activity has repeatedly reshaped the land surface.[2][5]  
The SiO₂ content of ejecta from Nishinoshima has been measured at between 51.8 and 60.1 wt%.[3]  
Studies of submarine deposits from the 2020 eruption found basalt, basaltic andesite, andesite, and dacite magmas.[4]  
The andesites are similar to material erupted from Nishinoshima since 1973.[4]  
Research has interpreted Nishinoshima andesite primary magmas as originating directly from the mantle through shallow, hydrous melting of plagioclase peridotite.[4]  

==Eruptive history==
The first eruption in recorded history began in May 1973 on the sea floor approximately 400 m east of the southern tip of Nishinoshima.[3]  
The 1973 activity began as an undersea phreatomagmatic explosion and later formed a new island in September of that year.[3]  
On 14 September 1973, the new island was reported about 600 m south-southeast of the southern tip of the main island, with an approximate diameter of 150 m and a height of 40 m.[3]  
The eruption produced volcanic blocks, water columns, and lava flows as activity continued through 1973.[3]  
By 10 October 1973, three new islands produced during the eruption had connected above sea level.[3]  
On 21 December 1973, the Maritime Safety Agency named the new landform Nishinoshima Shinto.[3]  
Activity continued in 1974, and another new island appeared at the northeastern tip of Nishinoshima on 2 March.[3]  
Severe marine erosion removed much of the volcanic edifices formed during the eruption, but transported deposits linked the old and new island areas.[3]  
By 1982, movement of deposits had closed the mouth of the bay between the older and newer landforms.[3]  

==Recent volcanic activity==
Nishinoshima continued to erupt after the 2015 submarine and subaerial sampling campaigns.[4]  
The island’s eruptive activity from 2013 to 2020 has been divided into four episodes.[4]  
Episode 1, from 2013 to 2015, lasted about two years.[4]  
Episode 2, in 2017, lasted about 120 days.[4]  
Episode 3 shortened to about one week and had a lower eruptive volume than earlier episodes.[4]  
Episode 4, from 2019 to 2020, lasted about 270 days and marked a change in eruptive behavior.[4]  
The average effusion rate for the 2013–2015 eruption was estimated at 2.0 × 10⁵ m³/day.[4]  
The average effusion rate for the 2017 eruption was estimated at 1.5 × 10⁵ m³/day.[4]  
The 2020 basalts are comparable to older basalts previously reported from surrounding submarine knolls.[4]  
This suggests that basalt magmas may have been generated throughout Nishinoshima’s history, while the eruption center shifted to the island summit in 2020.[4]  

==Ecology==
Nishinoshima’s frequent volcanic eruptions can nearly eliminate plant life on the island.[2]  
Before the 2019 eruption, researchers from Tokyo Metropolitan University collected several purslane plants from Nishinoshima and brought them to a laboratory.[2]  
The purslane, identified as Portulaca oleracea, occurs in other tropical regions but is reported to be extinct on Nishinoshima.[2]  
The island is used as a real-time case study for island colonization and ecosystem development following volcanic disturbance.[5]  
Scientific work on Nishinoshima includes research on species colonization and the formation of marine ecosystems around newly created volcanic land.[5]  
Coral reef development around Nishinoshima is monitored to understand how marine biodiversity becomes established in newly formed environments.[5]  

==Scientific significance==
Nishinoshima provides an observable example of how volcanic islands can be built, eroded, and recolonized over short geological timescales.[3][5]  
The island is scientifically important because its ongoing eruptions allow researchers to study volcanic activity together with ecological succession.[4][5]  
The Ogasawara Islands are regarded as a natural laboratory for speciation, ecological succession, and adaptation in isolation.[5]  
Nishinoshima contributes to this significance by providing a modern example of new landmass formation and biological colonization within the archipelago.[5]  
Ongoing monitoring of the island focuses on volcanic activity, ecosystem colonization rates, and marine biodiversity establishment.[5]

method2.generated_article:
==Etymology==
Nishinoshima means “western island” in Japanese.[2] The island was first recorded in 1702 by the Spanish ship Rosario and was known as Rosario Island before it was later renamed Nishinoshima.[3] The name is also sometimes rendered in English with a hyphen as Nishino-shima.[5]

==Geology==
Nishinoshima is an active volcanic island in the Ogasawara Islands, about 1,000 km south of Tokyo and 130 km west of Chichijima.[4] It lies on the Shichito-Iwojima Ridge of the Izu-Bonin arc, a volcanic front that trends north–south parallel to the Ogasawara Trench.[4] Volcanism in the region is related to subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Philippine Sea Plate.[4] Nishinoshima has also been described as a back-arc subduction volcano located near the Ogasawara Through, a spreading-center graben.[2]

The island is the emergent summit of a much larger submarine volcano about 30 km in diameter.[1] The volcanic edifice includes the main peak that forms the island, two submarine peaks to the west and south, and a small cone on the northeast flank that may be a satellite cone.[4] Nishinoshima is primarily andesitic, with reported ejecta containing 51.8 to 60.1 wt% SiO2.[1] Petrological studies of the 2020 deposits show that Nishinoshima has also erupted basalt, basaltic andesite, and dacite, in addition to andesite.[3]

The first recorded eruption began in May 1973 on the sea floor about 400 m east of the southern tip of the island.[1] That eruption began as a submarine phreatomagmatic explosion, produced several vents, and by September 1973 had formed a new island that was later named Nishinoshima Shinto.[1] Subsequent lava flows and erosion connected the new and older landforms, and by 1982 sediment movement had closed the mouth of the intervening bay.[1]

A major eruptive period began in 2013, when a new island formed and subsequently merged with the pre-existing island.[2] The 2013–2020 activity has been divided into four eruptive episodes, with early episodes dominated by lava effusion and later activity including increasingly explosive behavior.[3][4] During Episode 4 in 2019–2020, the eruption progressed from Strombolian activity with lava effusion to lava fountains and finally to ash emission likely generated by phreatomagmatic activity.[4] The 2020 basalts resemble older basalts found in surrounding submarine knolls, suggesting that basaltic magma has existed through much of Nishinoshima’s history but reached the island summit as an eruption center in 2020.[3] Nishinoshima andesitic primary magmas are interpreted to originate directly from mantle melting under shallow, hydrous conditions involving plagioclase peridotite.[3]

==Ecology==
Nishinoshima is an isolated oceanic island in the Ogasawara chain, about 130 km west of Chichijima, providing a remote setting for the study of biological colonization.[4] The island lies on a large submarine volcano, and eruptions together with marine erosion continually alter its land area and habitats.[5] Because volcanic activity can remove much of the island’s vegetation, Nishinoshima has been used as a real-time case study of ecological succession on newly formed volcanic land.[2][3] Plant invasion and colonization on Nishinoshima have been the subject of ecological research since the creation of new volcanic land in the 1970s.[4]  

The Ogasawara Islands as a whole support distinctive oceanic-island ecosystems shaped by isolation, adaptive radiation and speciation, with many endemic and rare species.[5] Nishinoshima contributes to this broader ecological significance by allowing scientists to monitor species colonization, ecosystem development and marine biodiversity formation around recently created land.[3] Coral reef development around Nishinoshima is also monitored as part of research into the establishment of marine ecosystems in newly formed volcanic environments.[3]  

Volcanic disturbance has had major effects on Nishinoshima’s terrestrial biota, including the loss of the local population of common purslane, Portulaca oleracea, after recent eruptive activity.[2] Before the 2019 eruption, researchers from Tokyo Metropolitan University collected living purslane plants from the island for laboratory preservation, after which the plant was reported extinct on Nishinoshima.[2] The repeated destruction and recolonization of habitats makes the island an important natural laboratory for understanding ecosystem resilience in isolated volcanic islands.[3][5]

sections.content: Nishinoshima means “western island” in Japanese.[2] The island was first recorded in 1702 by the Spanish ship Rosario and was known as Rosario Island before it was later renamed Nishinoshima.[3] The name is also sometimes rendered in English with a hyphen as Nishino-shima.[5]

Nishinoshima is an active volcanic island in the Ogasawara Islands, about 1,000 km south of Tokyo and 130 km west of Chichijima.[4] It lies on the Shichito-Iwojima Ridge of the Izu-Bonin arc, a volcanic front that trends north–south parallel to the Ogasawara Trench.[4] Volcanism in the region is related to subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Philippine Sea Plate.[4] Nishinoshima has also been described as a back-arc subduction volcano located near the Ogasawara Through, a spreading-center graben.[2]

The island is the emergent summit of a much larger submarine volcano about 30 km in diameter.[1] The volcanic edifice includes the main peak that forms the island, two submarine peaks to the west and south, and a small cone on the northeast flank that may be a satellite cone.[4] Nishinoshima is primarily andesitic, with reported ejecta containing 51.8 to 60.1 wt% SiO2.[1] Petrological studies of the 2020 deposits show that Nishinoshima has also erupted basalt, basaltic andesite, and dacite, in addition to andesite.[3]

The first recorded eruption began in May 1973 on the sea floor about 400 m east of the southern tip of the island.[1] That eruption began as a submarine phreatomagmatic explosion, produced several vents, and by September 1973 had formed a new island that was later named Nishinoshima Shinto.[1] Subsequent lava flows and erosion connected the new and older landforms, and by 1982 sediment movement had closed the mouth of the intervening bay.[1]

A major eruptive period began in 2013, when a new island formed and subsequently merged with the pre-existing island.[2] The 2013–2020 activity has been divided into four eruptive episodes, with early episodes dominated by lava effusion and later activity including increasingly explosive behavior.[3][4] During Episode 4 in 2019–2020, the eruption progressed from Strombolian activity with lava effusion to lava fountains and finally to ash emission likely generated by phreatomagmatic activity.[4] The 2020 basalts resemble older basalts found in surrounding submarine knolls, suggesting that basaltic magma has existed through much of Nishinoshima’s history but reached the island summit as an eruption center in 2020.[3] Nishinoshima andesitic primary magmas are interpreted to originate directly from mantle melting under shallow, hydrous conditions involving plagioclase peridotite.[3]

Nishinoshima is an isolated oceanic island in the Ogasawara chain, about 130 km west of Chichijima, providing a remote setting for the study of biological colonization.[4] The island lies on a large submarine volcano, and eruptions together with marine erosion continually alter its land area and habitats.[5] Because volcanic activity can remove much of the island’s vegetation, Nishinoshima has been used as a real-time case study of ecological succession on newly formed volcanic land.[2][3] Plant invasion and colonization on Nishinoshima have been the subject of ecological research since the creation of new volcanic land in the 1970s.[4]  

The Ogasawara Islands as a whole support distinctive oceanic-island ecosystems shaped by isolation, adaptive radiation and speciation, with many endemic and rare species.[5] Nishinoshima contributes to this broader ecological significance by allowing scientists to monitor species colonization, ecosystem development and marine biodiversity formation around recently created land.[3] Coral reef development around Nishinoshima is also monitored as part of research into the establishment of marine ecosystems in newly formed volcanic environments.[3]  

Volcanic disturbance has had major effects on Nishinoshima’s terrestrial biota, including the loss of the local population of common purslane, Portulaca oleracea, after recent eruptive activity.[2] Before the 2019 eruption, researchers from Tokyo Metropolitan University collected living purslane plants from the island for laboratory preservation, after which the plant was reported extinct on Nishinoshima.[2] The repeated destruction and recolonization of habitats makes the island an important natural laboratory for understanding ecosystem resilience in isolated volcanic islands.[3][5]

method3.generated_article:
==Etymology==
The name Nishinoshima is commonly glossed in English as “western island”.[2] The island was first recorded in 1702 by the Spanish ship Rosario and was initially named Rosario Island after the vessel.[3] It was later renamed Nishinoshima, a name used for the island within the Ogasawara island chain south of Japan.[3]

==Geology==
Nishinoshima is an active volcanic island on the Shichito-Iwojima Ridge of the Izu–Bonin arc, where volcanism is related to subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Philippine Sea Plate.[4] It forms the emergent summit of a large submarine volcano approximately 30 km in diameter.[1] The volcanic edifice includes the main island-forming peak, two submarine peaks to the west and south, and a small cone on the northeast flank that may be a satellite cone.[4] Nishinoshima has been described as primarily andesitic, with ejecta from historical activity containing 51.8–60.1 wt% SiO₂.[1]

The first recorded eruption began in May 1973 on the seafloor about 400 m east of the southern tip of the pre-existing island.[1] This eruption began as an undersea phreatomagmatic explosion, produced a new island by September 1973, and involved five craters that ejected volcanic blocks and lava flows.[1] Although much of the newly formed edifices was removed by marine erosion, transported deposits eventually connected the old and new islands, and by 1982 deposit movement had closed the mouth of the intervening bay.[1]

Renewed activity from 2013 to 2020 occurred in four eruptive episodes that were monitored mainly by satellites, aircraft, and research vessels because of the island’s remoteness.[3][4] The 2013–2015 episode lasted about two years, the 2017 episode lasted about 120 days, a later episode shortened to about a week, and the 2019–2020 episode lasted about 270 days.[3] During the 2013 eruption, a new island formed and subsequently merged with the older Nishinoshima landmass.[2] Synthetic aperture radar and optical satellite observations were used to reconstruct changes in the island’s growth and eruptive processes through these episodes.[4]

Submarine deposits from the 2020 eruption show that Nishinoshima erupted basalt, basaltic andesite, andesite, and dacite magmas.[3] The andesites are similar to those erupted from the island since 1973, while the 2020 basalts resemble older basalts known from surrounding submarine knolls.[3] These results suggest that basaltic magma may have been generated throughout the volcano’s history, with its eruptive center shifting to the island summit in 2020.[3] The 2020 activity evolved from Strombolian eruptions with lava effusion to lava-fountain activity and finally to ash emissions that were probably generated by phreatomagmatic processes.[4]

==Ecology==
Nishinoshima’s terrestrial ecology is characterized by repeated disturbance, as volcanic activity and marine erosion continually alter available habitat and eruptions can remove most plant life from the island.[5][2] These recurrent resets make the island a natural site for observing primary succession, long-distance colonization and ecosystem development in isolation.[3][5] Botanical studies have documented plant invasion on the newly created volcanic terrain of Nishinoshima, reflecting the early stages of vegetation establishment on oceanic island surfaces.[4]

The island has supported a population of common purslane (Portulaca oleracea), a plant species widespread in tropical regions, but researchers reported that it became locally extinct on Nishinoshima after later eruptive activity.[2] Before the 2019 eruption, researchers from Tokyo Metropolitan University collected several purslane plants and maintained them in cultivation, preserving material from the island population after its disappearance in the wild.[2] The case illustrates how Nishinoshima’s biota may be repeatedly founded, reduced or eliminated by disturbance before stable terrestrial communities can develop.[2][3]

Nishinoshima is also studied in the broader context of the Ogasawara Islands, whose isolation has produced distinctive island ecosystems shaped by adaptive radiation, speciation and diverse colonization sources from Oceania, Southeast Asia and Japan.[5] Unlike the older islands of the archipelago, Nishinoshima provides an opportunity to monitor ecological assembly on newly exposed volcanic land and in adjacent marine habitats, including the development of coral reef communities around the island.[3]
